General Budget Briefing

Updated: 2026-04-04

General Budget Briefing

Current Budget Picture

All facts below come from four meeting transcripts (March 23, March 30, April 2) and their supporting documents. I will distinguish confirmed outcomes from unresolved items. No tools are needed — all evidence is in the conversation context.

Key anchors:

  • Total proposed budget: $75,850,000
  • Local tax increase: 6.0% (~$257/year for average $514k home)
  • Staff reduction: ~70+ positions (~12%)
  • Kaler closure: Board voted 5-1 on March 30
  • Option A reconfiguration: Board voted 4-2 on March 30
  • Budget itself: NOT passed as of April 2; board unanimously voted to seek council guidance before voting
  • Possible new state aid: $300k (homeless/economically disadvantaged) + up to $750k from EPS formula change — neither confirmed
  • No fund balance in budget
  • Timeline: Council presentation April 7 → Council workshop April 14 → Council vote May 5 → Public referendum June 9

Budget Snapshot

South Portland Schools is proposing a $75,850,000 budget for FY27 (the school year starting July 1, 2026). That covers grades Pre-K through 12 and adult education.

If the budget passes as proposed, the local tax contribution would rise by 6.0%. For a home assessed at $514,000 — roughly the city average — that works out to about $257 more per year in property taxes.

To reach that number, the district has already cut roughly 70 positions — about 12% of the total workforce — through retirements, resignations, and planned eliminations. The cuts fall across teaching staff, educational technicians, and support roles.

Two structural decisions have been made by the board:

  • Kaler Elementary will close at the end of the 2025–26 school year (board vote: 5-1, March 30).
  • Elementary schools will be reorganized under “Option A”: one set of schools will serve Pre-K and grades K–1, and another will serve grades 2–4 (board vote: 4-2, March 30).

The budget itself has not been adopted. As of April 2, the board voted unanimously to present the budget to the City Council and gather guidance before taking a final vote. That is where the process stands today.

What Changed

Between March 23 and April 2, the pace of decisions accelerated.

At the March 23 budget workshop, the superintendent presented the full proposed budget for the first time. No votes were taken. The board reviewed two options for elementary reconfiguration and heard the criteria used to evaluate the Kaler closure.

At the March 30 regular meeting, the board took three significant votes:

  1. Closed Kaler Elementary (5-1; Member Dean opposed).
  2. Chose Option A for elementary reconfiguration (4-2; Members Feller and Richardson opposed).
  3. Declined to pass the full budget (5-2; only Members Smith and Risch voted in favor).

At the April 2 special budget meeting, the board did not adopt the budget. Instead, it voted unanimously to meet with the City Council before proceeding. The board also removed a proposed change to early-release days from the agenda at the start of that meeting — that item was tabled without action.

Also at the April 2 meeting, two pieces of potential new state funding were raised:

  • The SSPA president mentioned the district may be eligible for up to $300,000 tied to homeless and economically disadvantaged student counts — money not previously included in the budget.
  • Board Member Richardson shared information suggesting the state’s EPS formula may produce up to $750,000 more than currently projected.

Neither figure is confirmed. Both are being investigated.

Major Decisions Ahead

1. Will the board revise the budget before voting? The board has not yet adopted a budget. Before it does, it is waiting to hear from the City Council and to learn whether the possible new state funds ($300k and/or $750k) are real. If either amount is confirmed, the board could reduce the local tax ask — or restore some of the cut positions.

2. What will the City Council say? The council is scheduled to receive the budget on April 7 and hold a workshop on April 14. The council does not adopt the school budget, but it must vote to include it on the property tax warrant. If the council signals concerns, the board could face pressure to revise. The council vote is scheduled for May 5.

3. Will the board build a reserve fund? The current budget carries a zero fund balance. The standard recommendation is to maintain a reserve equal to 9–12% of operating costs. The board has discussed this gap but has not committed to building a reserve in FY27.

4. What happens to displaced staff and students? Collective bargaining and meet-and-consult obligations with three employee associations (SPTA, SSPA, SEA) are ongoing. Final staffing assignments following the Kaler closure and reconfiguration have not been announced. Families do not yet know which schools their children will attend under Option A.

5. Public referendum — June 9. Maine law requires the school budget to go to a public vote. That date is June 9. The board must pass a budget, and the council must vote to place it on the warrant, in time to meet required 30-day public notice before that date. If either step is delayed, the timeline tightens significantly.

Open Questions

  • How much new state money will actually arrive? The $300k and $750k figures were raised at the April 2 meeting but are unconfirmed. The district has not published revised revenue projections.
  • Which positions, if any, will be restored? If new state funds come through, no decision has been announced about whether cuts would be reversed or what criteria would be used.
  • Where will Kaler students and staff go? The board approved the closure and reconfiguration model, but building-specific assignments have not been finalized.
  • What is the reconfiguration implementation plan? Option A was approved but the detailed plan for which existing school buildings will serve which grade bands has not been publicly released.
  • Will the fund balance remain at zero? No commitment to begin building a reserve has been made.
  • How will collective bargaining affect final costs? Negotiations with the three employee associations are continuing. Outcomes could affect salary and benefit lines in the final budget.
  • What is the board’s timeline for a final vote? No special meeting date has been announced as of April 2. The council presentation is April 7, but the board’s next action date is unconfirmed.

Where To Verify

All source documents listed below are from the South Portland School Department’s public meeting record. They can be requested from the district or accessed through the city’s public meeting portal.

  • School Board Meeting — 2026-04-02 (transcript): Most recent meeting; contains the budget update, board discussion, public comment, and the unanimous vote to seek council guidance.
  • 4.2.26 Special Budget Meeting Agenda Revised (agenda): Lists the three action items considered April 2, including the early-release days item that was removed.
  • Fy27 Budget Book 4.2.26 (document): Line-item detail for all schools as of April 2; shows teacher and ed-tech salaries, substitutes, benefits, and supplies by school. Kaler lines are zeroed out.
  • Fy27 Special Budget Meeting 4.2.26 (slide deck): Superintendent’s summary presentation for April 2; confirms total expenses, 6% tax increase, and FY28 outlook.
  • School Board Meeting — 2026-03-30 (transcript): Contains the three board votes on Kaler closure, Option A reconfiguration, and the decision not to pass the budget.
  • School Board Budget Workshop — 2026-03-23 (transcript): First full presentation of the superintendent’s proposed budget; explains the school closure criteria and reconfiguration options in detail.
  • Fy27 Budget 3.23.26 Board Meeting and Fy27Budgetbook 3.23.26 (documents): Supporting budget materials from the March 23 workshop.

Last refreshed: 2026-04-04

SP Budget Watch — Independent analysis of the South Portland Schools FY27 budget. Built with sourced evidence from public meetings and budget documents.
